title: Day 4 (1/2) — GPT, transformers & scaling
summary: Part 1 of 2 · Steps 1–14 · §1–§3 lecture. Next: **Day 4 (2/2)** for tokens, lab & costs.

sections.heading: What GPT stands for
sections.remember: **GPT** = **G**enerative **P**re-trained **T**ransformer — a model that predicts the next **token** using a **transformer** trained on large data.
points: **Generative** — output is generated token-by-token (next-token prediction), not a single fixed label.
**Pre-trained** — weights are learned on large corpora first; your API call uses that base model (plus any product tuning).
**Transformer** — the **architecture** (how layers are wired); Step 2 explains why it matters.

sections.heading: Transformer (the T in GPT)
sections.remember: A **transformer** uses **self-attention** on **sequences** so each token can use context from earlier tokens — that enabled scaling to modern **LLMs**.
points: Comes from the 2017 paper *Attention Is All You Need* — an **architecture**, not just more data.
**Self-attention** — when processing one token, the model learns which other tokens in the sequence to weight.
Why it matters for you: efficient training at scale → large **parameters**, lower cost per capability vs older sequence models.
Internals (layers, decoders) come later in the course; today = purpose and vocabulary only.

visual.sentence: The cat sat on the mat

sections.heading: Tokens (overview)
sections.remember: The model reads **token IDs**, not raw English — billing and **context window** are counted in **tokens** (deep dive in **Day 4 Part 2**).
points: **Tokenization** = text → list of integer **token IDs** before the neural net runs.
Lab preview: `tiktoken` in `week1/day4.ipynb` (Part 2 Steps 9–10).

visual.text: Hi my name is Ed and I like banoffee pie

sections.heading: Stateless API & the messages list
sections.remember: Each `chat.completions` call is **stateless** — you must pass the full **messages** array every time; continuity is **context**, not server memory.
points: The API does not store prior turns; a new HTTP request starts with no hidden history.
**messages** = `[{role, content}, ...]` — typically **system**, **user**, **assistant** roles for each turn.
The model runs **next-token prediction** on the entire serialized conversation you send in that request.
If an earlier turn said “I'm Ed” and you omit it from `messages`, the model cannot infer it — notebook demo in **Part 2**.
**ChatGPT**-style products resend the growing thread each message → illusion of **memory**; you pay tokens for all of it.

left.label: Incomplete context
lines: No prior turn with the name

right.label: Full context
lines: messages: [system, user: I'm Ed, assistant: Hi Ed!, user: What's my name?]

sections.heading: Before transformers — LSTM
sections.remember: **LSTM** (long short-term memory), a **recurrent neural network**, modeled sequences deeply but could not **parallelize** training like transformers.
points: Many researchers thought LSTMs understood sequence relationships **better** than early transformers.
**RNN/LSTM** flow: process timestep-by-step; earlier outputs feed the next step → hard to run all steps **in parallel** → slow, expensive to scale.
**Transformer** trade-off: simpler mechanism (**attention**), massively **parallel** training → scale won over “more clever” recurrence.
Paper title *Attention Is All You Need* = you may not need all the RNN complexity if **attention** plus scale is enough.

sections.heading: Hype, stochastic parrots & emergent behavior
sections.remember: LLMs only optimize **likely next tokens** — yet at scale, outputs are often **correct**; that gap is called **emergent** capability and still surprises experts.
points: **2023** — public shock at ChatGPT; then backlash (e.g. **“Stochastic Parrots”** paper): warning not to treat statistical text as guaranteed truth.
Not surprising: realistic **plausible** continuations (good “predictive text”).
Surprising: plausible tokens are often **factually right** (e.g. correct **math** answers) — **why it generalizes** still debated.
**Emergent intelligence** (informal): large networks → outputs **imitate** intelligent responses, not just fluent ones.

sections.heading: Prompt engineering → everyday skill
sections.remember: **Prompt engineering** = crafting the **input sequence** (role, context, style, examples) so the **output tokens** match your task.
points: Techniques: state the **task**, give **background**, specify **tone/format**, use few-shot examples.
Was briefly a high-paid specialty; now a normal skill with **ChatGPT**, **Claude**, etc.
Core for **AI engineers** — you design what goes into `messages` and system prompts.

sections.heading: Copilots — human + LLM in the workflow
sections.remember: **Copilots** embed an LLM beside your work (**GitHub Copilot**, **Microsoft Copilot**) — automate drudge tasks and enrich documents/code collaboratively.
points: A **product pattern**: LLM + editor/IDE + context from your file or app.
You stay in the loop: accept, edit, or reject suggestions (supervision again).

sections.heading: Context engineering
sections.remember: **Context engineering** = designing **everything in the input** (prompt + retrieved data + tool results) so the model’s next-token prediction fits the real task.
points: Think beyond one clever sentence — whole **context window** is your lever (Part 2 §7).
Example: ticket prices must **appear in the input** if the answer must quote current fares.
Includes **tools** and **RAG** (later weeks) — still fundamentally better **input sequence** → better output.

sections.heading: Agentic AI & autonomy
sections.remember: **Agentic AI** ≈ an **LLM in a loop** with **tools** — the model chooses **what to do next** by writing tokens that trigger actions.
points: **LLM controls the workflow** — may call other models, **tools**, or functions.
**LLM in a loop** — repeated calls; e.g. **Claude Code**, **GPT agent** (Day 3).
**Autonomy**: output tokens can include “next step” instructions — still **stateless** each HTTP call.

sections.heading: What are parameters?
sections.remember: **Parameters** are the learned numbers inside the network — classic ML used tens; modern LLMs use **millions to trillions**.
points: Traditional ML: ~**20–200 parameters** for credit scoring from ~20–30 features.
**Deep learning**: millions or billions of weighted connections; tuned during **training**.
Deeper video explainers in course resources if you want more.

sections.heading: Parameter growth & “more with less”
sections.remember: Historically **more parameters** ≈ more capability — **better training** now beats older giants at smaller sizes.
points: Milestones: **GPT-1** ~117M → **GPT-2** ~1.5B → **GPT-3** ~175B → **GPT-4** ~1.76T (reported; frontier counts often **undisclosed**).
**Gemma** ~270M can outperform **GPT-2** despite fewer parameters.
Tiers: **GPT-5** nano/mini/full; **Claude** Haiku/Sonnet/Opus — larger ≈ more parameters ≈ higher cost.

sections.heading: Training-time scaling
sections.remember: **Training-time scaling** = bigger model + more training data — expensive upfront, stored in the weights.
points: **Chinchilla scaling laws**: balance **parameter count** and **training tokens**.
Bigger models cost more to **train** and usually more per token to **run**.
Open-weight examples: **Llama**, **GPT OSS**, **DeepSeek**.

sections.heading: Inference-time scaling
sections.remember: **Inference-time scaling** = smarter behavior **at run time** — reasoning, richer **context**, **RAG**, tools (no retrain).
points: Examples: **reasoning models**, **budget forcing** (`wait`), facts in the **input**.
Combine with training-time scaling: large model + strong context setup.
**MoE** (mixture of experts): huge total parameters; only some experts fire per token.

cheatSheet: API = stateless · full messages[] each call
